en.extract:
The long-tailed paradise whydah or eastern paradise whydah (Vidua paradisaea) is a bird in the family Viduidae of the order Passeriformes. It is a small passerine with a short, stubby bill, found across eastern and southern Africa from Ethiopia south to South Africa and west to Angola. It is mostly granivorous, and feed on seeds that have ripened and fallen on the ground. The ability to distinguish between males and females is quite difficult unless it is breeding season. During this time, the males moult into breeding plumage where they have a distinctive long tail. This can be up to three times longer than its own body or even more. Males are able to mimic songs where females can use that to discover their mate. However, there are some cases where females don't use songs to choose their mate but they use either male characteristics like plumage or they can have a shortage of options with song mimicry. Paradise whydahs are brood parasites. They do not destroy the host eggs that are originally there, but lay their own eggs alogside in the other bird's nest. Overall, these whydahs are considered least concern, based on the IUCN Red List of threatened species.


Taxonomy and systematics
The long-tailed paradise whydah was formally described by the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus in 1758 in the tenth edition of his Systema Naturae under the binomial name Emberiza paradisaea. It is now placed in the genus Vidua that was introduced by the French naturalist Georges Cuvier in 1816.
The long-tailed paradise whydah is a brood-parasitic bird like the rest of the species in the family Viduidae. The family diverged about 20 million years ago. The primary host species are in the family Estrildidae, also known as the waxbills. Most have included Viduidae within Estrilididae or Ploceidae (weavers) in a subfamily of its own. Similarly, Anomalospiza have been switched around between the two families and have not been linked with Vidua. However, studies has shown that the skull, the bony palate, the horny palate and the pterylosis are some of the morphological characters that support a close relationship between Anomalospiza and Vidua which are different from the weavers. Indigobirds are also part of the family Viduidae. The long-tailed whydah's relationship with the indigobirds are not well known. The indigobirds are more closely related to the straw-tailed whydah based on their phylogenetic relationship where researchers analysed mitochondrial restriction sites and nucleotide sequences.


Description
Breeding male long-tailed paradise whydahs have black heads and back, a rusty coloured breast, a bright buffy-yellow nape, and white abdomen with broad, elongated black tail feathers that can grow up to 36 cm or more. When in non-breeding plumage, males are hard to distinguish from females, and in general, from other Viduidae species, as these differ from one another in size, in non-breeding plumage and colour, and in the songs used for mating.. Usually, they are about 13 cm in length and weigh about 21 grams. Females tend to have a grey bill and feathers that are greyish-brown with blackish streaks along with their under tail feather being more white. Males during the non-breeding season tend to have mostly browner plumage with black stripes on the crown, black parts along the face, and deeper brown colour for the chest and creamer colour for the abdomen. 


Distribution and habitat
The long-tailed paradise whydahs are found in grassland, savanna and open woodland where they live in bushed grassland around cultivation. The majority of the time, these whydahs stay away from surface waters.


Behaviour and ecology
The long-tailed paradise whydah are known to be brood parasites where they would lay their eggs in nests of other songbirds. Furthermore, they usually roost together in flocks during both breeding and non-breeding seasons. Males develop the ability to mimic songs of their host. Studies showed that female whydahs respond more strongly to songs mimicked by males of their own species than they do to closely related species. Females use this mimicry to eliminate among potential mates and prefer those raised by the same host species. Hybrids can occur with other related paradise whydahs; researchers discovered that hybridisation can occur when female whydahs do not choose mates based on their song mimicry but instead on male traits such as plumage and flight displays if it is more important to them than song, or is restricted by the availability of males singing the appropriate host songs or if males is involved with unsolicited copulation with females of other parasitic species. Researchers discovered that these paradise whydahs mimic the songs of Melba Finch.

Additionally, these paradise whydahs are granivorous where they feed on small seed that ripen and fall on the ground. For foraging, they use something called "double scratch" where they use both of their feet almost simultaneously scratching the ground to find seeds in dust and hop backwards to pick up the seed. Another technique they use is their tongue. They dehusk grass seeds with their bill by rolling the seeds with their tongue one at a time back and forth against the ridge of the palate.


Relationship to humans
Whydahs in general have been kept for many years as cage birds, for their song and brightly coloured breeding plumage. In 1581, a renaissance scholar named Michel de Montaigne visited Florence where he was able to see these paradise whydahs in the Medici aviaries. He described them with la cue deus longues plumes comme celles d'un chapon which in translation meant "a tail of two long plumes like those of a rooster". Ligozzi, a chief botanical painter of the Medici aviaries, illustrated a painting of the common fig where people later identified that the two birds in the painting were actually the paradise whydah and the indigobird. Other than the beauty, the paradise whydahs can be a nuisance especially for farmers. For instance, in the highlands of Guinea and Sierra Leone, paradise whydahs feed on small seeds of cultivated fonio before they can be harvested, and that also happens to be the first food source available to the human inhabitants after the season of rains.


Status
Widespread throughout its large range, the long-tailed paradise whydah is evaluated as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species.


References


External links

BirdLife Species Factsheet

The '''long-tailed paradise whydah''' or '''eastern paradise whydah''' ('''''Vidua paradisaea''''') is a bird in the family [[Viduidae]] of the order [[Passeriformes]]. It is a small passerine with a short, stubby bill, found across eastern and southern Africa from [[Ethiopia]] south to [[South Africa]] and west to [[Angola]]. It is mostly granivorous, and feed on seeds that have ripened and fallen on the ground. The ability to distinguish between males and females is quite difficult unless it is breeding season. During this time, the males moult into breeding [[plumage]] where they have a distinctive long tail. This can be up to three times longer than its own body or even more. Males are able to mimic songs where females can use that to discover their mate. However, there are some cases where females don't use songs to choose their mate but they use either male characteristics like plumage or they can have a shortage of options with song mimicry. Paradise whydahs are brood parasites. They do not destroy the host eggs that are originally there, but lay their own eggs alogside in the other bird's nest. Overall, these whydahs are considered least concern, based on the IUCN Red List of threatened species.

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The long-tailed paradise whydah was [[Species description|formally described]] by the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] in 1758 in the [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|tenth edition]] of his ''[[Systema Naturae]]'' under the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] ''Emberiza paradisaea''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1758 | title= Systema Naturae per regna tria naturae, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | volume=1 | edition=10th | page=178 | publisher=Laurentii Salvii | location=Holmiae (Stockholm) | language=Latin | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/727085 }}</ref> It is now placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Vidua]]'' that was introduced by the French naturalist [[Georges Cuvier]] in 1816.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Cuvier | first=Georges | author-link=Georges Cuvier | year=1816 | title=Le Règne animal distribué d'après son organisation: pour servir de base a l'histoire naturelle des animaux et d'introduction a l'anatomie comparée | volume=1 | language=fr | place=Paris | publisher=Déterville | pages=388–389 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/1844898 }}</ref>

The long-tailed paradise whydah is a brood-parasitic bird like the rest of the species in the family Viduidae. The family diverged about 20 million years ago.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last=John|first=Roy|date=2011-01-01|title="Handbook of the Birds of the World. Volume 15. Weavers to New World Warblers" edited by Josep del Hoyo et al. 2008. [book review]|journal=The Canadian Field-Naturalist|volume=125|issue=1|page=83|doi=10.22621/cfn.v125i1.1139|issn=0008-3550|doi-access=free}}</ref> The primary host species are in the family [[Estrildidae]], also known as the waxbills. Most have included Viduidae within Estrilididae or [[Ploceidae]] (weavers) in a subfamily of its own.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Chapin|first=James P.|date=October 1929|title=Nomenclature and Systematic Position of the Paradise Whydahs|journal=The Auk|volume=46|issue=4|pages=474–484|doi=10.2307/4076183|issn=0004-8038|jstor=4076183|doi-access=free}}</ref> Similarly, ''[[Anomalospiza]]'' have been switched around between the two families and have not been linked with ''Vidua''.<ref name=":1" /> However, studies has shown that the skull, the bony palate, the horny palate and the [[pterylosis]] are some of the morphological characters that support a close relationship between ''Anomalospiza'' and ''Vidua'' which are different from the weavers.<ref name=":0" /> Indigobirds are also part of the family Viduidae. The long-tailed whydah's relationship with the indigobirds are not well known. The indigobirds are more closely related to the [[straw-tailed whydah]] based on their [[Phylogenetics|phylogenetic]] relationship where researchers analysed mitochondrial restriction sites and nucleotide sequences.<ref name=":0" />

==Description==
Breeding male long-tailed paradise whydahs have black heads and back, a rusty coloured breast, a bright buffy-yellow nape, and white abdomen with broad, elongated black tail feathers that can grow up to 36 cm or more.<ref name=":0" /> When in non-breeding plumage, males are hard to distinguish from females, and in general, from other Viduidae species, as these differ from one another in size, in non-breeding plumage and colour, and in the songs used for mating.<ref name=":0" />. Usually, they are about 13 cm in length and weigh about 21 grams.<ref name=":0" /> Females tend to have a grey bill and feathers that are greyish-brown with blackish streaks along with their under tail feather being more white.<ref name=":0" /> Males during the non-breeding season tend to have mostly browner plumage with black stripes on the crown, black parts along the face, and deeper brown colour for the chest and creamer colour for the abdomen.<ref name=":0" />

File:Long-tailed Paradise Whydah (Vidua paradisaea) (17329851342).jpg|Male in breeding plumage
File:Vidua paradisaea-20081223B.jpg|Female
</gallery>

== Distribution and habitat ==
The long-tailed paradise whydahs are found in grassland, savanna and open woodland where they live in bushed grassland around cultivation.<ref name=":0" /> The majority of the time, these whydahs stay away from surface waters.<ref name=":0" />

== Behaviour and ecology ==
The long-tailed paradise whydah are known to be [[brood parasite]]s where they would lay their eggs in nests of other [[songbird]]s.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Payne|first1=Robert B.|last2=Payne|first2=Laura L.|last3=Woods|first3=Jean L.|last4=Sorenson|first4=Michael D.|date=January 2000|title=Imprinting and the origin of parasite–host species associations in brood-parasitic indigobirds, Vidua chalybeata|journal=Animal Behaviour|volume=59|issue=1|pages=69–81|doi=10.1006/anbe.1999.1283|pmid=10640368|s2cid=22363915|issn=0003-3472}}</ref> Furthermore, they usually roost together in flocks during both breeding and non-breeding seasons.<ref name=":0" /> Males develop the ability to mimic songs of their host.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=PAYNE|first1=ROBERT B|last2=PAYNE|first2=LAURA L|last3=WOODS|first3=JEAN L|date=June 1998|title=Song learning in brood-parasitic indigobirdsVidua chalybeata: song mimicry of the host species|journal=Animal Behaviour|volume=55|issue=6|pages=1537–1553|doi=10.1006/anbe.1997.0701|pmid=9641999|s2cid=17693319|issn=0003-3472}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last1=Payne|first1=Robert B.|last2=Sorenson|first2=Michael D.|date=2004|journal=The Auk|volume=121|issue=1|page=156|doi=10.1642/0004-8038(2004)121[0156:bagioa]2.0.co;2|issn=0004-8038|title=Behavioral and Genetic Identification of a Hybrid Vidua: Maternal Origin and Mate Choice in a Brood-Parasitic Finch|s2cid=84524204 }}</ref> Studies showed that female whydahs respond more strongly to songs mimicked by males of their own species than they do to closely related species.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Behavior and Songs of Hybrid Parasitic Finches.|last=Payne, Robert B.|date=1980|oclc=869799441}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Payne|first=Robert B.|date=January 1973|title=Behavior, Mimetic Songs and Song Dialects, and Relationships of the Parasitic Indigobirds (Vidua) of Africa|journal=Ornithological Monographs|issue=11|pages=ii–333|doi=10.2307/40166751|issn=0078-6594|jstor=40166751|s2cid=84118406}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Payne|first=R.B.|date=November 1973|title=Vocal mimicry of the paraside whydahs (Vidua) and response of female whydahs to the songs of their hosts (Pytilia) and their mimics|journal=Animal Behaviour|volume=21|issue=4|pages=762–771|doi=10.1016/s0003-3472(73)80102-2|issn=0003-3472}}</ref> Females use this mimicry to eliminate among potential mates and prefer those raised by the same host species.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Oakes|first1=Edward J.|last2=Barnard|first2=Phoebe|date=October 1994|title=Fluctuating asymmetry and mate choice in paradise whydahs, Vidua paradisaea: an experimental manipulation|journal=Animal Behaviour|volume=48|issue=4|pages=937–943|doi=10.1006/anbe.1994.1319|s2cid=53195903|issn=0003-3472}}</ref> [[Hybrid (biology)|Hybrid]]s can occur with other related paradise whydahs; researchers discovered that hybridisation can occur when female whydahs do not choose mates based on their song mimicry but instead on male traits such as plumage and flight displays if it is more important to them than song, or is restricted by the availability of males singing the appropriate host songs or if males is involved with unsolicited copulation with females of other parasitic species.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Grant|first1=Peter R.|last2=Grant|first2=B. Rosemary|date=January 1997|title=Hybridization, Sexual Imprinting, and Mate Choice|journal=The American Naturalist|volume=149|issue=1|pages=1–28|doi=10.1086/285976|s2cid=83665115|issn=0003-0147}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Barnard|first=Phoebe|date=April 1990|title=Male tail length, sexual display intensity and female sexual response in a parasitic African finch|journal=Animal Behaviour|volume=39|issue=4|pages=652–656|doi=10.1016/s0003-3472(05)80376-8|s2cid=54339389|issn=0003-3472}}</ref> Researchers discovered that these paradise whydahs mimic the songs of Melba Finch.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":2" />
[[File:Long-tailed Paradise-Whydah (Vidua paradisea) and Cut-throat Finches (Amadina fasciata) (6046350546).jpg|left|thumb|Long-tailed paradise whydah foraging for seeds on the ground]]
Additionally, these paradise whydahs are granivorous where they feed on small seed that ripen and fall on the ground.<ref name=":0" /> For foraging, they use something called "double scratch" where they use both of their feet almost simultaneously scratching the ground to find seeds in dust and hop backwards to pick up the seed.<ref name=":0" /> Another technique they use is their tongue. They dehusk grass seeds with their bill by rolling the seeds with their tongue one at a time back and forth against the ridge of the palate.<ref name=":0" />

== Relationship to humans ==
Whydahs in general have been kept for many years as cage birds, for their song and brightly coloured breeding plumage.<ref name=":0" /> In 1581, a renaissance scholar named [[Michel de Montaigne]] visited Florence where he was able to see these paradise whydahs in the Medici aviaries.<ref name=":0" /> He described them with ''la cue deus longues plumes comme celles d'un chapon'' which in translation meant "a tail of two long plumes like those of a rooster".<ref name=":0" /> Ligozzi, a chief botanical painter of the Medici aviaries, illustrated a painting of the common fig where people later identified that the two birds in the painting were actually the paradise whydah and the indigobird.<ref name=":0" /> Other than the beauty, the paradise whydahs can be a nuisance especially for farmers. For instance, in the highlands of [[Guinea]] and [[Sierra Leone]], paradise whydahs feed on small seeds of cultivated [[fonio]] before they can be harvested, and that also happens to be the first food source available to the human inhabitants after the season of rains.<ref name=":0" />

== Status ==
Widespread throughout its large range, the long-tailed paradise whydah is evaluated as Least Concern on the [[IUCN Red List]] of Threatened Species.

zh.extract:
乐园维达雀（台湾作天堂維達雀，学名：Vidua paradisaea），是雀形目维达雀科的一种鸟类。


特征
乐园维达雀体重约20.35克，翼长约78.6毫米，喙宽度约5.2毫米，喙厚度约6.9毫米，嘴峰长约12.1毫米，跗蹠长约16毫米，尾长约59.7毫米。
乐园维达雀是留鸟，栖息在林地，它们是植食性动物，主要以植物的种子或坚果为食。它们分布在苏丹东部、埃塞俄比亚、索马里南部，南至南非东北部，西至安哥拉和纳米比亚北部。
